praise: Excellent build quality - massively built, well-engineered yacht noted as 'one of the most gorgeous and most gorgeously engineered yachts'
Sails exceptionally well, especially to windward with cutter rig configuration
Exceptional tracking ability from well-proportioned skeg-hung rudder and moderate-draft fin keel
Outstanding comfort for extended ocean cruising - ideal layout with centre cockpit, large aft cabins, generous storage
Superior deck layout designed for safe offshore sailing with shorthanded crew; all control lines led aft
Deck saloon provides light, airy interior far superior to traditional 'cave-like' designs
Proven bluewater cruiser - numerous documented Atlantic crossings and world cruising pedigree
Professional support and strong resale value from original builder (Oyster Marine)
complaints: Maintenance costs can be high due to complex systems (electric winches, furling systems)
Teak deck replacement is a major capital expenditure (~$70,000 documented estimate; $60-120k range typical)
Deck moisture ingress issues from aging teak screw bungs and caulking in 25+ year old examples
Stainless steel chainplates susceptible to crevice corrosion where they pass through deck
Some owners in Mediterranean report covering all deck saloon windows to manage heat gain
Electric furling motors and gearboxes require specialized maintenance and corrosion checks
Heavily-used examples show significant depletion and poor maintenance - used market shows many boats run hard and not well-kept
Premium pricing compared to similar boats (e.g., Hallberg-Rassy at similar price points)
known_issues: Deck teak moisture ingress - bungs and caulking fail over time, requiring thorough moisture meter testing
Upper shroud chainplate crevice corrosion where chainplate passes through deck under loading
Mild steel keel bolts susceptible to weeping/corrosion at hull joint requiring inspection
In-mast furling system motors and gearboxes susceptible to corrosion in tropical climates
Deck saloon window sealing degradation, especially in tropical/Mediterranean cruising
Standing rigging due for inspection/replacement on 25+ year old examples (past 10-15 year industry standard)
Potential bow thruster electrical/mechanical wear on heavily-used examples

removed.reason: Record claims 17.46 (or 16.66); ListingsPort — the primary confirmed accessible source — gives 13.69. The discrepancy of ~30% is material. The two claimed source URLs (sailseek.org returned ECONNREFUSED; sailboat.guide returned ECONNREFUSED) could not be verified. The value 17.46 appears fabricated or drawn from incorrect data; 13.69 from the only accessible spec sheet is more plausible for a heavy displacement cruiser of this type.

Claimed 'closed-cell structural foam core for strength and insulation' — both primary cited sources (boats.com returned HTTP 403; Oyster heritage page made no mention of insulation or foam core). No accessible source confirms this specific construction detail. Generic structural claim presented with construction-specific precision that has no verifiable basis.

Claimed as 'a core design element' with 0.9 confidence — both cited sources blocked (boats.com 403; Oyster heritage page silent on this). The Oyster heritage page, the most authoritative accessible source, does not mention watertight bulkheads. This is an important safety claim that cannot be verified and should not be assumed.

Sole cited source (CruisersForum thread) returned HTTP 403 and could not be accessed. Claim about 'some owners in Mediterranean have reported covering all deck saloon windows' has no verifiable source backing.

Oyster heritage page returned no content supporting 'spacious owner's stateroom aft with centerline berth feels light and airy'; framing as a quality assessment rather than documented fact; partially overlaps with verified ventilation fact. Confidence 0.8 is overstated without source access.

Cited source (Practical Boat Owner windvane guide) is a generic windvane article with no Oyster 485-specific content. The claim 'some owners have installed Hydrovane or Monitor windvanes post-purchase' has no boat-specific source. Confidence was already 0.5 — below acceptable threshold given the generic source.

Both cited sources blocked (boats.com 403; sailboat.guide ECONNREFUSED). The claim of 'skeg-hung rudder on full skeg, raked design tucked well under stern' is plausible for this era but unverifiable from accessible sources.

Both cited sources blocked (boats.com 403; Oyster heritage page silent on this). Specific claim of 'aluminum toe rail, solid stainless steel handrails, and teak decking' is plausible but unconfirmed by any accessible source.

Sole cited source (boats.com Perry review) returned HTTP 403. 'SV Break of Dawn (Oyster 485)' is a specific vessel name with specific heavy-weather documentation — this level of specificity requires accessible source confirmation that could not be obtained.

Both cited sources returned HTTP 403 (yachtall.com) and HTTP 403 (CruisersForum). The specific claim of 'Orange Storm staysail documented' is a suspiciously precise detail with no accessible source. While cutter rigs are plausible for Oyster 485s, the specific details and the named staysail cannot be verified.

Downgraded from removed — kept in cleaned_facts but with substantially reduced confidence (0.55) and hedged wording, because the Lighthouse Yachting source is accessible and describes the deck saloon, though it does not confirm a dedicated inside helm station. The original claim of 'Yes - inside helm position with weather shelter' is not supported.

fabrication_flags: stability.sad_ratio: Value of '17.46 (or 16.66)' contradicts the only accessible spec source (ListingsPort: 13.69). A 30% inflation in SA/D ratio significantly mischaracterizes boat performance. Both claimed source URLs were unreachable (ECONNREFUSED). This number appears to be fabricated or drawn from a different vessel.

construction.displacement: Record claims '17,690 kg (39,000-41,000 lbs)'. The only accessible spec source gives 37,550 lbs / 17,032 kg — roughly 1,500-3,500 lbs lighter than the range claimed. The broad range '39,000-41,000 lbs' with no single consistent figure is a flag for aggregated invention. Primary sources (sailboatdata.com, Oyster heritage) were inaccessible (403/no data), leaving this unresolved but flagged.

tropical.refrigeration: Specific model designation 'DC75' for Grunert refrigerator has no source support — Swiftsure confirms Grunert brand but gives no model number; Seattle Yachts confirms Grunert brand with no model. 'DC75' appears to be a plausible-sounding but invented model designation.

tropical.watermaker: Claimed '40-60 gal/hr capacity'. Only confirmed figure from Seattle Yachts listing is 40 gal/hr. The upper bound of 60 gal/hr appears to be an unsourced extension. YachtAll (second source) returned 403.

heavy_weather.cutter_staysail: 'Orange Storm staysail documented' — this exact color/type designation has no accessible source. YachtAll returned 403. The specificity of a named sail color without any accessible source is a fabrication flag.

refit.standing_rigging_age: Record claims '10-15 year replacement cycle'. The Riggtech source actually states 7-10 years, making the record's claim directly contradicted by its own cited source. The inflated 10-15 year window understates urgency for aging 485s.

self_steering.electrical_autonomy: Claim presented as a general model characteristic ('8x 6V AGM house batteries typical') when it is documented only on one specific vessel (Swiftsure Discovery). The Seattle Yachts 1995 example uses an entirely different LiFePo4 architecture. Generalizing one boat's refit to a model-level claim is a methodological fabrication.
verify.verdict: Partially verified with significant concerns. Core identity facts (designer Holman and Pye, production 1992-2002, ~36 units, capsize screening 1.67, D/L 318, ballast ratio 34.62%) are confirmed by accessible sources. However, four facts are materially wrong or contradicted: (1) SA/D ratio is 13.69 not 17.46 — a 30% inflation that mischaracterizes performance; (2) displacement of 39,000-41,000 lbs is overstated vs. 37,550 lbs from the accessible spec sheet; (3) standing rigging replacement cycle is 7-10 years not 10-15 years per the cited source itself; (4) the 'Orange Storm staysail' and 'SV Break of Dawn' are specific claims whose sole sources are inaccessible (403). Equipment facts (Webasto/Eberspaecher heating, Grunert fridge, Seawater Pro watermaker, Northern Lights generator) are source-supported but only at the individual-example level — they describe specific boats, not model-standard equipment. Structural/safety claims (watertight bulkheads, foam-core insulation, inside helm) lack any accessible source confirmation and were removed. The record's confidence of 0.82 is substantially overstated; 0.62 reflects that roughly one-third of facts either lack accessible sources, contain specific numbers contradicted by sources, or generalize individual-boat retrofits to model-level characteristics.

cold_score.rationale: Three of seven cold-weather inputs have accessible source support. Heating scores 18.0/25 (Webasto on Swiftsure listing, Eberspacher on Seattle Yachts, confidence 0.72 — individual examples, not universal factory spec). Inside/sheltered helm scores 6.6/20 (deck saloon documented as providing shelter in unfavorable conditions, but no dedicated inside helm station confirmed, confidence 0.55, partial credit). Keel-stepped mast scores 7.5/10 (in-mast furling on keel-stepped aluminium mast confirmed, confidence 0.75). Insulation (15 pts) removed — sole sources were inaccessible (403). Watertight bulkheads (10 pts) removed — no accessible source. Hard cockpit shelter (10 pts) and cold tankage (10 pts) absent from verified facts. Re-normalised over known 55 pts: 32.1/55 * 100 = 58. Confidence equals coverage: 55/100 = 0.55.
cold_score.basis: Heating: swiftsureyachts.com/oyster-485-discovery (Webasto); seattleyachts.com listing (Eberspacher). Helm shelter: lighthouseyachting.com deck saloon listing. Keel-stepped mast: listingsport.com/sailboats/oyster/485.

tropic_score.rationale: Four of six tropical inputs have accessible source support. Ventilation/opening hatches scores 17.0/20 (deck saloon ventilation and oversized opening hull ports documented, confidence 0.85). Refrigeration scores 11.25/15 (Grunert 24V water-cooled and 120V units on multiple examples, confidence 0.75 — individual retrofits, not a single model-standard unit). Watermaker scores 4.2/15 (Seawater Pro 40 gal/hr documented on one 1995 example only; not model-standard, confidence 0.70 discounted by 0.40 individual-example factor). Light-air SA/D scores 3.75/20 (SA/D 13.69 from ListingsPort — only accessible spec; at D/L 318 this is poor light-air performance, below the 14 threshold for moderate-air ability; score 5/20 pre-confidence at confidence 0.75). Shade (15 pts) removed — sole source inaccessible. Airflow (15 pts) removed — sole source inaccessible. Re-normalised over known 70 pts: 36.2/70 * 100 = 52. Confidence equals coverage: 70/100 = 0.70.
tropic_score.basis: Ventilation: lighthouseyachting.com; seattleyachts.com. Refrigeration: swiftsureyachts.com; seattleyachts.com. Watermaker: seattleyachts.com. SA/D: listingsport.com/sailboats/oyster/485 (13.69).

stability_score.rationale: The rubric accepts only AVS (Angle of Vanishing Stability) or CE Design Category for the stability score. Neither is present in the verified facts: AVS is explicitly listed as a gap ('no specific value found despite multiple searches'), and CE category is also listed as a gap ('referenced as inshore-capable but exact category not specified'). The capsize screening value (1.67) is verified from ListingsPort — a value below 2.0 is favourable — and the ballast ratio (34.62%) and D/L ratio (318) are positive structural indicators, but the rubric does not score on these metrics. Assigning a non-zero score from unscored proxies would violate the rubric. No fabrication.
stability_score.basis: Not published. AVS and CE category both listed as unresolved gaps in verified facts. CSF 1.67 from listingsport.com/sailboats/oyster/485 is confirmed but out-of-rubric.

storm_verdict.text: Insufficient sourced data. The sole heavy-weather field report identified during research — referencing SV Break of Dawn (an Oyster 485) and a Perry Design Review — was sourced from boats.com, which returned HTTP 403 during verification and could not be accessed. No alternative forum threads or passage reports with Oyster 485-specific storm experience were accessible to the verification agent. The structural indicators available from verified sources (CSF 1.67, ballast ratio 34.62%, D/L 318, keel-stepped mast, lead keel) are consistent with a stiff, heavy-displacement bluewater design, but these are design proxies — not documented storm performance. No assertion of heavy-weather behaviour can be made from these facts alone.

refit_projection.assumptions: Baseline vessel: 1992-2002 production run (approximately 24-34 years old in 2026); median-year assumption is 1997 (~29 years). Low end assumes: rigging recently replaced, engine recently serviced with low hours, teak deck in repairable condition (not full replacement), watermaker present and functional, electronics partially updated. High end assumes: all systems at or beyond service-life end, teak deck requiring complete professional replacement (verified $60k-$120k range from NewMilMarine source), Perkins M90 requiring full repower, all electronics replaced, and watermaker absent. Teak deck is the single largest variable and dominates the high-end estimate — its condition is the most critical pre-purchase survey item. Engine range reflects major service ($5k) at low end vs. full repower ($60k) at high end given the ~29-year-old Perkins M90 sitting at the edge of the 25-30yr repower threshold. Standing rigging range uses $10k-$20k per verified Riggtech/yachtservice.vip sources (not the rubric default $8k, which is contradicted by verified data). All figures are our estimates; a professional survey is required before purchase.

by_item.note: 7-10yr industry cycle; 29yr boat is on 3rd+ set. Verified source range $10k-$20k for 45-55ft vessel. In-mast furling rig complicates access.
8-10yr cycle; budget full replacement of halyards, sheets, control lines.
8-12yr sail life. In-mast furling eliminates roach, reducing mainsail area vs. standard battened main — prices at lower end of rubric range. Include furling genoa/jib and storm sails.

Low assumes recent service, good hours: fluids, filters, impeller, belts, heat exchanger anode service. High assumes full repower at 29yr vintage sitting at 25-30yr threshold. Parts widely available (confirmed). Repower to modern 75-80hp diesel.

by_item.system: Electronics (chart plotters, VHF, AIS, radar, instruments)
by_item.note: 10-15yr cycle; 29yr boat almost certainly on original or first-generation updated electronics. Full replacement of navigation suite.
6-10yr cycle. Configuration varies widely by example (AGM bank vs LiFePO4 as documented on two verified listings). Budget for house bank replacement; solar/alternator upgrade at high end.
Condition-dependent, not age-dependent. Assume worn windlass and chain on 29yr vessel.
25yr replacement cycle; 29yr boat is past cycle. All seacocks warrant survey; budget full replacement.
Offshore passage preparation: liferaft (service or replace), EPIRB, flares, tethers, jacklines, harnesses.
10-15yr canvas life. Deck saloon Oysters typically carry full cockpit enclosure; budget replacement.

Verified from NewMilMarine source: $200-$400/sq ft professional replacement; $60k-$120k for a 40ft yacht — Oyster 485 at 48ft will be at or above upper end. This is the single most consequential item. Confirmed known issue: failing screw bungs and caulking on 25+ year examples. Pre-purchase moisture meter survey is essential.
Crevice corrosion on upper shroud chainplates is a documented known issue per ListingsPort. Replacement with 316L stainless plus re-bedding.

by_item.system: Electric in-mast furling motor/gearbox (Hood or Selden)
by_item.note: Documented known issue: motors and gearboxes require specialized maintenance. Cleaning and re-lubrication at low end; motor/gearbox replacement at high end. Tropical use accelerates corrosion.

by_item.system: Deck seals and deck saloon windows
by_item.note: Deck saloon window resealing, teak screw bung replacement, deck core seals. Deck moisture ingress is a confirmed known issue.

by_item.system: Keel bolt inspection and remediation (mild steel — known issue)
by_item.note: Mild steel keel bolts susceptible to weeping/corrosion at hull joint per ListingsPort. Haul-out inspection required; budget for re-bedding or partial replacement if weeping found.
Documented on one example (Seawater Pro 40 gal/hr); not model-standard equipment. If absent or beyond service life, budget full installation.

provisioning_specific.item: Teak deck moisture survey — hire a marine surveyor with a calibrated moisture meter
provisioning_specific.why: Teak deck moisture ingress from failing screw bungs and caulking is the highest-cost confirmed known issue. Full replacement is $60k-$120k+. Survey determines whether repair or replacement is needed before committing to purchase price.

provisioning_specific.item: Keel bolt inspection — haul-out and professional inspection of mild steel keel bolts
provisioning_specific.why: Mild steel keel bolts are a confirmed known issue per ListingsPort. Weeping or corrosion at the hull-keel joint is an offshore safety concern and may require re-bedding or bolt replacement.

provisioning_specific.item: Chainplate inspection — removal and inspection of upper shroud chainplates for crevice corrosion
provisioning_specific.why: Crevice corrosion on upper shroud chainplates where they pass through the deck under loading is a confirmed known issue. Chainplate failure at sea is catastrophic.

provisioning_specific.item: Electric in-mast furling system service log review
provisioning_specific.why: Hood or Selden in-mast furling motors and gearboxes require specialized maintenance and are susceptible to corrosion. Request service history; if none, budget $1.5k-$5k for inspection and service before departure.

provisioning_specific.item: Perkins M90 engine hours and service records — verify against repower threshold
provisioning_specific.why: At ~29 years old the Perkins M90 sits at the 25-30yr repower threshold. If hours are high or service records are absent, budget $25k-$60k for repower. Parts are confirmed widely available, but rebuild costs are non-trivial.

provisioning_specific.item: Standing rigging dye-penetrant or ultrasonic inspection — replace if over 7-10 years old
provisioning_specific.why: Verified industry standard is 7-10yr replacement (not 10-15yr as sometimes claimed). A 29yr boat may be on its third or fourth standing rig; any rig of unknown age should be replaced before bluewater passage.

provisioning_specific.item: In-mast furling sail inventory — carry a separate removable forestay and storm jib
provisioning_specific.why: In-mast furling eliminates the ability to fully deploy a battened main in a blown-out condition and jams under heavy load. A removable inner forestay with a hanked-on storm jib provides independent storm canvas not dependent on the electric furling system.

provisioning_specific.item: Spare furling motor and gearbox components
provisioning_specific.why: Electric in-mast furling is a single-point failure for the mainsail. Parts for Hood and Selden systems are not universally available in offshore ports. Carry motor brushes, gearbox lubricant, and the manual-override handle.

provisioning_specific.item: Heat exchanger anodes — Perkins M90 internal corrosion is a known issue
provisioning_specific.why: ListingsPort confirms Perkins Range4 M90 heat exchangers are prone to internal corrosion without regular anode replacement. Carry a full set of zincs and inspect at every engine service.

provisioning_specific.item: Deck saloon UV and heat management for tropical passages
provisioning_specific.why: The large deck saloon windows that provide excellent light and ventilation in temperate climates become significant solar heat gain surfaces in the tropics. Budget for removable UV-blocking interior shades or external awning panels — sentiment data notes Mediterranean owners covering saloon windows to manage heat.

provisioning_specific.item: Manual bilge pump accessible from cockpit with handle permanently installed
provisioning_specific.why: With electric in-mast furling, electric autopilot, and complex electrical systems documented across examples, a manual backup bilge pump accessible from the cockpit (not relying on the electrical system) is essential for offshore.

provisioning_specific.item: Watermaker service kit and pre-filter spares
provisioning_specific.why: Watermaker documented on one example (not model-standard). If fitted, carry membrane flush solution, pre-filter cartridges, and a high-pressure pump rebuild kit. A 40 gal/hr unit at 29yr vintage will need membrane inspection before bluewater use.
